summary:
The Rashtrakuta Dynasty (753–982 AD) was the dominant power of the Deccan plateau in medieval India — a dynasty of apparently Kannada origin that ruled much of central India and repeatedly raided north and south, winning a reputation as the greatest military power of its time. Arab travelers described the Rashtrakuta king as one of the four great kings of the world (alongside the caliphs of Baghdad, the Tang emperor of China, and the Byzantine emperor). The dynasty's control of the Deccan gave it strategic position between the northern Indian plains and the Tamil south.

The Rashtrakutas are most celebrated for commissioning the Kailasa Temple at Ellora ([[event:2174|Ellora Caves]]) — the most extraordinary rock-cut temple in the world, carved top-down from a single basalt cliff face to create a free-standing temple complex two stories tall and the size of the Parthenon, completed under Krishna I (c. 756–773 AD). The sheer engineering ambition of carving an entire temple from solid rock — working from the top down, removing an estimated 200,000 tons of stone — makes the Kailasa Temple one of the supreme feats of ancient construction. The Rashtrakutas were generally tolerant of multiple religions — they patronized Jain scholars alongside Hindu temples — and their court was a major center of Sanskrit and Kannada literature. The dynasty fell to a rebellion by its feudatories, the Chalukyas of Kalyani, in 982 AD.
